fr_chablis_william_fevre_montee_de_tonne.appearance: Pale lemon with glowing green-gold highlights; brilliant clarity. Jasper Morris notes 'a glowing lemon and lime colour' (2023 vintage) and 'pale but glowing' with 'as much yellow as white but balanced' (2022). Medium viscosity consistent with 12.5% ABV.
fr_chablis_william_fevre_montee_de_tonne.nose_summary: Intensely mineral-driven nose with prominent white flower aromas (acacia blossom), citrus confit, lemon zest, and orchard fruit. Distinctive Kimmeridgian seashore notes: iodine, shellfish, oyster shell, and saline/algae character. Secondary notes of crushed limestone, wet stones, oatmeal, and chalky reduction. Burghound highlights 'white flowers, prominent mineral reduction and plenty of seashore elements'; CellarTracker notes 'crushed limestone, sappy orchard fruits, chalk dust, salty sea air with a little crushed oyster funk.'
fr_chablis_william_fevre_montee_de_tonne.palate_summary: Medium to full-bodied, satiny and layered with excellent mid-palate density. Crisp orchard fruit and citrus (lemon oil, citron confit) supported by a chiselled white fruit intensity. Sappy texture with evident minerality and stoniness. William Kelley (Robert Parker, 94 pts) describes it as 'fleshy and seamless, with fine depth at the core.' Finish is long, driving, firm, and impeccably balanced with pungent stoniness and good acidity at the back.
fr_chablis_william_fevre_montee_de_tonne.structural_summary: Acid: high, racy, providing excellent tension and backbone. Body: medium to medium-plus. Alcohol: 12.5%, modest and well-integrated. Tannin: n/a (white). Finish: long to very long, with persistent mineral and stony character. Oak is restrained -- partial seasoned French oak only, no new oak -- adding subtle textural richness without overt oak flavour.
sources: William Kelley, Robert Parker Wine Advocate (94 pts, 2020 vintage)
Jasper Morris MW, Inside Burgundy (92-95 pts, 2022 vintage; 94-96 pts, 2023 vintage)
Allen Meadows, Burghound (92-94 pts, 2022 vintage)
Decanter (95 pts, 2022 vintage)
CellarTracker community notes (2017 vintage, 94 pts)

fr_macon_louis_latour_les_genievres.appearance: Pale yellow to pale gold with brilliant clarity and youthful vibrancy. The 2023 tech sheet describes 'pale yellow in colour'; the 2024 vintage shows 'a brilliant pale gold colour.' Light to medium viscosity consistent with the fresh, early-drinking style at 13% ABV.
fr_macon_louis_latour_les_genievres.nose_summary: Fresh and fruit-forward nose centred on citrus (grapefruit, green lemon) and orchard fruit (ripe apple, pear). The 2023 vintage reveals aromas of muscatel and brioche with yellow peach notes. The 2024 shows floral, expressive aromas of acacia and honeysuckle with delicate vanilla. Older vintages add subtle honey and nutty notes. Sommeliers Choice Awards (91 pts, 2022) notes 'ripe apple with a hint of spice, citrus, orchard fruits, and rocky minerals.'
fr_macon_louis_latour_les_genievres.palate_summary: Ample and round on the palate with generous stone fruit and citrus character. Yellow peach (2023) and yellow fruit (2024) dominate, with muscat-like aromatics on the mid-palate. Medium-bodied with a suave, approachable texture. Best Buy Liquors describes it as 'opulent, rich, with a flowery bouquet and lovely honey, apple and nutty flavours.' Finish shows nice freshness and good length for the appellation, though not extended. Wine Spectator (2023) calls it 'fresh and rather steely.'
fr_macon_louis_latour_les_genievres.structural_summary: Acid: medium to medium-plus, providing freshness without austerity. Body: medium. Alcohol: 13%, well-balanced. Tannin: n/a (white). Finish: medium length with clean, fresh citrus-mineral close. No oak influence -- 100% stainless steel fermentation and ageing for 8-10 months. Cellaring potential: 2-3 years.
sources: Maison Louis Latour official tech sheet (2023 and 2024 vintages)
James Suckling (90 pts, 2023 vintage)
Sommeliers Choice Awards (91 pts, 2022 vintage)
Wine-Searcher critic aggregation
Wine on Sale / Best Buy Liquors retailer tasting notes (2022 and 2024 vintages)

fr_puligny_montrachet_olivier_leflaive_v.appearance: Bright, shiny lemon-yellow of medium intensity with hints of a gold hue. Social Vignerons describes the 2014 as 'a bright and shiny lemon-yellow color of medium intensity, although made deeper by hints of a gold hue.' Good clarity and moderate viscosity consistent with barrel-fermented Burgundy at 13% ABV.
fr_puligny_montrachet_olivier_leflaive_v.nose_summary: Noble and distinguished aromas combining citrus (lemon, candied lemon), stone fruit (peach, apricot), and delicate florals (acacia blossom, lily, elderflower). Pronounced mineral character with toasted and nutty notes from barrel fermentation -- sour dough, toasted nuts, buttery brioche. Subtle oak spice and hints of caramel provide depth. ReserveBar notes 'beautiful minerality and toasted scents'; Social Vignerons highlights 'punchy notes of dough and toasted nuts as dominant aromas.' Wilson Daniels (Esprit Leflaive) describes 'ripe stone fruit and citrus with subtle saline notes, flint, and fresh white flowers.'
fr_puligny_montrachet_olivier_leflaive_v.palate_summary: Dry, mineral-driven palate with crisp acidity providing sustained tension. Oily-enough texture from lees ageing, yet lean and focused. Bursts of subtle tropical fruit (pineapple, mango) backed by rich ripe peach, combined with powerful spicy oak notes. ReserveBar notes 'baked apple, peach, spiced pear, subtle pineapple and mango flavours, lemony acidity.' CellarTracker (2022) praises 'all the right peach/pear notes with crisp acidity and medium to long finish. Extremely balanced.' The finish is rather long and layered on sweet/savoury flavours with excellent persistence.
fr_puligny_montrachet_olivier_leflaive_v.structural_summary: Acid: medium-plus to high, providing crisp minerality and tension throughout. Body: medium, relatively light which aids approachability and purity of fruit expression. Alcohol: 13%. Tannin: n/a (white). Finish: medium-long with good persistence. Oak: 100% French oak (circa 20% new) for 12 months, plus 4-6 months stainless steel -- adds textural richness and subtle spice without dominating.
sources: Olivier Leflaive official website (2021 vintage tasting note)
Social Vignerons detailed review (2014 vintage)
ReserveBar retailer tasting notes
CellarTracker community notes (2022 vintage)
Wilson Daniels / Esprit Leflaive tech sheet

fr_gevrey_chambertin_faiveley_vieilles_v.appearance: Deep, clear ruby-red hue. Wine Spectator (2023 vintage) notes 'a pale ruby color' suggesting the 2023 is lighter; the Domaine describes 'a deep, clear ruby-red hue' as the house norm. Good clarity and moderate-plus viscosity typical of concentrated village Gevrey.
fr_gevrey_chambertin_faiveley_vieilles_v.nose_summary: Exceptionally intense and aromatic nose revealing ripe dark berries (black cherry, blackcurrant, boysenberry), sweet berries, and animalistic nuances. Robert Parker (2018, 90 pts) describes 'aromas of sweet berries, black truffle, and spices, framed by a subtle hint of oak.' Wine Spectator (2023, 92 pts) highlights 'cherry, strawberry, rose, mineral and spice flavours' with 'purity and finesse.' The Domaine's own notes emphasise 'ripe dark berries with animalistic nuances.' Earthy, iron-rich mineral undertones from clay-marl soils.
fr_gevrey_chambertin_faiveley_vieilles_v.palate_summary: Medium to full-bodied, fleshy, and enveloping with a concentrated, rich core. The 2018 Robert Parker note describes 'a concentrated, rich, and reserved core built around velvety tannins and lively acidity.' JJ Buckley (2022) notes 'intensely flavoured, featuring cherry, raspberry, boysenberry and earth flavours.' Wine Spectator (2023) praises 'excellent length and the impression of approachability' with 'deftly balanced' structure. Sweet spice, fleshy palate texture, notes of rich soil and black truffle. The Domaine highlights 'rich, powerful flavours' on the palate.
fr_gevrey_chambertin_faiveley_vieilles_v.structural_summary: Acid: medium-plus, lively, providing freshness and lift. Body: medium to full. Alcohol: 13%. Tannin: medium-plus, velvety to fine-grained with a light powdery dusting on the finish. Finish: long, well-defined, with lingering earth and spice. Oak: 14 months French oak, less than one-third new, with regular stirring -- subtle, not dominant.
sources: Robert Parker / Wine Advocate (90 pts, 2018 vintage)
Wine Spectator (92 pts, 2023 vintage; 93 pts, 2018 vintage)
James Suckling (93 pts, 2018 vintage)
Wilson Daniels / Domaine Faiveley official tech sheet
JJ Buckley retailer tasting note (2022 vintage)

us_willamette_domaine_drouhin_dundee_hil.appearance: Radiant red ruby hue. Veronique Drouhin-Boss describes the 2022 as displaying 'a radiant, red ruby hue.' The 2021 is noted as 'dark red colored' (Ken's Wine Guide). Medium viscosity; the wine appears lighter and more translucent than its 14.1% ABV might suggest.
us_willamette_domaine_drouhin_dundee_hil.nose_summary: Tight and delightful gathering of tart cherries, peony, pomegranate, red fruits, and wood spice (2022 tech sheet). The 2021 is 'bursting with black cherry and ripe sour cherry tones, with hints of dried violets and lightly roasted coffee beans.' Wine Enthusiast (94 pts, 2022) notes 'briary raspberries, graphite, earth and coffee with cream.' James Suckling (94 pts, 2022) finds 'spiced cherries, aged tangerine peel, crushed spices and nutshells, some licorice notes.' John Gilman (93 pts, 2022) describes 'cherries, sweet beetroot, woodsmoke, gamebird, soil tones, gentle stem tones and cedary oak.'
us_willamette_domaine_drouhin_dundee_hil.palate_summary: Balanced and clear acidity with customary elegance. The 2022 is 'medium to full-bodied, structured and polished with a firm backbone of very fine tannins and earthy undertones' (James Suckling). Wine Enthusiast notes it 'feels light on its feet, with a nice balance between gentle acidity and velvety tannins.' Ken's Wine Guide (2021, 94 pts) finds 'medium-bodied with integrated medium acidity, nicely balanced, smooth, and savoury' with 'black raspberry and light loam, notes of cherry-cola.' MyWinePal describes 'smooth and round with sweet red fruit, spice, and subtle oak.' The finish is lingering with fine tannins and earthy persistence.
us_willamette_domaine_drouhin_dundee_hil.structural_summary: Acid: medium-plus, well-integrated, providing Burgundian elegance. Body: medium to medium-full. Alcohol: 13.5-14.1% depending on vintage. Tannin: fine-grained, velvety, well-structured but not aggressive. Finish: medium-long to long with lingering earthy and fruit character. Oak: French oak (Burgundy coopers), about 20% new, for 12-14 months -- adds subtle cedar and spice framing.
sources: Domaine Drouhin Oregon official tech sheet / Veronique Boss-Drouhin tasting note (2022 vintage)
James Suckling (94 pts, 2022 vintage)
Wine Enthusiast (94 pts, Editors' Choice, 2022 vintage)
John Gilman / View from the Cellar (93 pts, 2022 vintage)
Ken's Wine Guide (94 pts, 2021 vintage)

nz_central_otago_felton_road_bannockburn.appearance: Medium-deep ruby with good intensity and clarity. The wine presents with more concentration and depth of colour than Burgundy equivalents, reflecting Central Otago's greater sun exposure. Screw-cap closure.
nz_central_otago_felton_road_bannockburn.nose_summary: Dense and richly aromatic with dark cherries, wild raspberries, mulberries, and blood plums. Exotic spice notes moving towards sumac, peppercorns, and Indian spices. Wineanorak (2023, 94 pts) describes 'wonderfully chiselled aromatics with sweet red cherries and plums, and a nice spicy, almost peppery twist.' Wine Advocate (2021, 92 pts) finds 'densely packed and powerful, with a cavalcade of red fruits, exotic spice and peppercorns.' Winepilot (2024, 97 pts) notes 'dark cherries, wild raspberries, mulberries, graphite, Indian spices and well-polished blood plums.' Wine Enthusiast (2019) highlights 'brambly berries, tree sap, graphite, baking spice, mushrooms and florals.' Saratoga Wine (2024) adds 'strawberries, orange peel, sandalwood and potpourri.'
nz_central_otago_felton_road_bannockburn.palate_summary: Spicy, exciting, and full with ripe fruit through the finish and a lick of raw cocoa. The 2023 has 'a rich, sweet core with bold, almost meaty cherry and raspberry fruit and some soft wild strawberry richness -- very seductive and more-ish, showing massive drinkability' (Wineanorak). Vinous (2022, 91 pts) describes it as 'pure, refined and lifted with a silky, smooth texture that caresses the mouth... sweet raspberry and cherry meet a finely tuned herby character.' Winepilot notes 'layer after layer of succulent fruit' with 'a foxy undergrowth character.' Finishes with spicy twist, cedar, and appetising textures.
nz_central_otago_felton_road_bannockburn.structural_summary: Acid: medium-plus, well-placed, providing freshness and lift. Body: medium to medium-plus, with notable concentration. Alcohol: 13.5-14%. Tannin: fine yet firm, sometimes chunky in richer vintages; talc-like and appetising in texture. Finish: medium-long to long with scented spice, cedar, and a sense of tension. Oak: 13 months French oak, circa 25% new -- well-integrated, adding polish without masking fruit. Biodynamic/organic farming shows in the purity and energy of the fruit.
sources: Wine Advocate / Joe Czerwinski (92 pts, 2021 vintage)
Vinous / Rebecca Gibb MW (91 pts, 2022 vintage)
Wineanorak / Jamie Goode (94 pts, 2023 vintage)
Winepilot / Lisa Cardelli (97 pts, 2024 vintage)
Wine Enthusiast / Christina Pickard (2019 vintage)

fr_pauillac_lynch_bages_grand_vin.appearance: Deep garnet to opaque blue-black with purple accents in youth. Wine Spectator (2020, No. 3 wine of 2023) notes the wine leads off with deep cassis and violet notes suggesting intense colour extraction. The Wine Cellar Insider describes '96-98... blue-black with purple accents.' Excellent depth and concentration visible in the glass.
fr_pauillac_lynch_bages_grand_vin.nose_summary: Pure creme de cassis and blackcurrant dominate, with layered notes of graphite, cedar, lead pencil, sweet tobacco, iron, and violet. Wine Spectator (2020) describes 'cassis and violet notes leading off, followed by additional waves of black cherry and blackberry fruit as well as sweet tobacco and iron, with subtle savory and cedar hints.' The 2022 shows 'powerful blackberry and boysenberry fruit, cedar and light tobacco notes.' The 2021 Wine Spectator (94 pts) notes 'caressing black cherry and black currant paste flavours, with warm cast iron, sweet tobacco.' James Suckling (99 pts, 2020) highlights 'a beautifully layered red with blackcurrant, hazelnut and lead-pencil character.'
fr_pauillac_lynch_bages_grand_vin.palate_summary: Full-bodied, rich, concentrated, and powerful yet with remarkable purity. The 2020 (Wine Spectator No. 3, 98 pts) is described as offering 'almost approachable fruit' with an 'iron note piercing through the fruit on the finish.' The Wine Cellar Insider notes the wine is 'rich and fat' with 'a refined, stern, masculine quality to the fruit with a finish that lasts and sustains the layers of fresh, sweet black currants.' Multiple vintages show the house style of power with polish -- Lynch-Bages consistently 'performs at a second-growth level' per Robert Parker. Cassis, graphite, and cedar persist through a very long finish.
fr_pauillac_lynch_bages_grand_vin.structural_summary: Acid: medium-plus, providing freshness and structure. Body: full. Alcohol: 13-14.1% depending on vintage. Tannin: firm and fine-grained to velvety, with structural backbone for decades of ageing. Finish: very long (50+ seconds in top vintages), with persistent cassis, cedar, and iron. Oak: 18 months French oak, approximately 75% new -- significant but well-integrated, contributing graphite, cedar, and toast complexity.
sources: Wine Spectator / James Molesworth (98 pts, 2020 vintage -- No. 3 Wine of 2023)
James Suckling (99 pts, 2020 vintage)
The Wine Cellar Insider (96-98 pts, 2019 en primeur)
Wine Spectator (94 pts, 2021 vintage)
Wine Spectator (2022 en primeur tasting note)

fr_saint_emilion_chateau_canon_grand_vin.appearance: Deep ruby with intense purple/violet hue and black core in youth. Tastingbook (2020) notes 'intense purple colour with violet hue and black core.' Jeb Dunnuck (2020) describes 'a deep ruby hue.' The Wine Cellar Insider notes Canon 2022 shows exceptional colour depth. Brilliant clarity with good legs reflecting 13.5-14.5% ABV.
fr_saint_emilion_chateau_canon_grand_vin.nose_summary: Extraordinarily pure and perfumed nose of red and black fruits (black raspberries, wild strawberries, darker cherries), pressed violets, iris, and spring flowers. Pronounced chalky minerality, crushed stones, and graphite -- the limestone plateau signature. Jeb Dunnuck (2022, 100 pts) notes 'ripe black cherries, graphite, crushed stone, and a beautiful floral character.' Wine Advocate (2020, 100 pts) describes 'aromas of cherries and raspberries mingled with notions of exotic spices and iris.' Neal Martin (Wine Advocate, 2019, 98 pts) highlights 'black raspberries, wild strawberries, spring flowers, chalky minerality, and graphite.' The Wine Cellar Insider adds 'mint, crushed stones, licorice, and spice.'
fr_saint_emilion_chateau_canon_grand_vin.palate_summary: Medium to full-bodied with seamless elegance, perfectly polished chalky tannins, and vibrant acids. The defining character is extraordinary purity and finesse rather than power. Wine Advocate (2020, 100 pts) describes it as 'deep and concentrated, with vibrant acids and beautifully polished, chalky tannins. Pure and precise, it concludes with a long, mouthwatering finish.' Vinous (2020, 98 pts) notes 'medium-bodied with fine tannins, fresh and almost citric, very vibrant and precise with a quite peppery finish.' The Wine Cellar Insider emphasises the 'polished silk profile' with 'layers of sensuous fruits, freshness and purity, finishing with a touch of salt.'
fr_saint_emilion_chateau_canon_grand_vin.structural_summary: Acid: medium-plus to high, vibrant, providing the essential freshness and limestone-driven energy. Body: medium to full. Alcohol: 13.5-14.5% depending on vintage. Tannin: ultra-fine, chalky, perfectly integrated -- the limestone plateau hallmark. Finish: very long to exceptionally long (50-60+ seconds in top vintages), with persistent mineral, salt, and pure red fruit. Oak: 18 months French oak, circa 50% new -- seamlessly integrated, never dominant.
sources: Jeb Dunnuck (100 pts, 2022 vintage -- No. 60 in Top 100 of 2025)
William Kelley, Wine Advocate (100 pts, 2020 vintage)
Jeb Dunnuck (97 pts, 2020 vintage)
Neal Martin, Wine Advocate (98 pts, 2019 vintage)
The Wine Cellar Insider (98-99 pts across 2019-2022 vintages)

fr_pessac_leognan_smith_haut_lafitte_rou.appearance: Deep garnet-purple to dark red, almost black in youth. Lisa Perrotti-Brown (2016) notes 'a deep garnet color'; Wine Advocate (2019) describes 'deep garnet-purple in color.' Millesima (2016) states 'a dark red color, almost black.' Opaque core with purple rim in youth. Excellent concentration visible in the glass.
fr_pessac_leognan_smith_haut_lafitte_rou.nose_summary: Intense and multi-layered nose led by blackcurrant (cassis), blackberry, and dark plum, with the diagnostic Graves smoky/graphite character distinguishing it from Medoc. James Suckling (2018, 98 pts) notes 'plum and cherry aromas, wet earth and spice, very floral and vivid.' Lisa Perrotti-Brown (2016, 99 pts) finds 'dramatic notes of creme de cassis, blueberry compote, and chocolate-covered cherries, plus star anise, lavender, and fallen leaves, with a touch of iron ore.' Wine Advocate (2019) describes 'blackcurrant preserves, stewed plums, juicy blackberries, candied violets, licorice, espresso, tapenade, crushed rocks and wood smoke.' The smoky/flinty mineral note is a recurrent Graves terroir marker across vintages.
fr_pessac_leognan_smith_haut_lafitte_rou.palate_summary: Full-bodied, rich, and opulent with polished, refined tannins and remarkable textural elegance. Wine Spectator describes the house style as 'a caressing wave of cassis, plum puree and blackberry compote flavours with enticing savoury, licorice, warm loam and mineral accents, with a cashmere finish.' Wine Advocate (2019) notes 'very firm, super-ripe, fine-grained tannins and bold freshness to support the voluptuous, mineral-accented fruit, finishing very long and achingly perfumed.' James Suckling (2018) praises 'layers of ripe tannins that are balanced and polished, incredibly polished and refined -- goes on for minutes.' The K&L (2021) note highlights 'velvety and seamless, deep and impressively multidimensional.'
fr_pessac_leognan_smith_haut_lafitte_rou.structural_summary: Acid: medium-plus, providing freshness and energy -- 'a certain sinewy, mineral quality that is quintessential Graves' (Jancis Robinson). Body: medium-full to full. Alcohol: 13.5-14.5%. Tannin: firm but fine-grained, polished, often described as 'cashmere' or 'silky' -- substantial but elegant. Finish: very long, with persistent smoky graphite, mineral, and dark fruit character. Oak: 18 months French oak (from in-house cooperage), approximately 60% new -- significant but integrated, contributing toast, cedar, and spice complexity. Organic/biodynamic practices since the Cathiard era.
sources: James Suckling (98 pts, 2018 vintage)
Lisa Perrotti-Brown / Wine Independent (99 pts, 2016 vintage)
Wine Advocate / Lisa Perrotti-Brown (97+ pts, 2019 vintage)
Wine Spectator / James Molesworth (house style description)
Millesima (2016 vintage detailed tasting note)
